facts.text: Mythology refers to the body of traditional stories a culture uses to explain the world, its origins, heroes, and gods.
Greek mythology's twelve Olympians include Zeus, Hera, Athena, Apollo, Artemis, Ares, Aphrodite, Hermes, Demeter, Poseidon, Hephaestus, and either Hestia or Dionysus.
Roman mythology borrowed heavily from Greek mythology, renaming gods like Zeus to Jupiter and Aphrodite to Venus.
Norse mythology centers on gods such as Odin, Thor, Loki, and Freyja and includes the world-tree Yggdrasil.
Ragnarok in Norse myth describes a final cosmic battle followed by renewal of the world.
Egyptian mythology includes major figures like Ra, Osiris, Isis, Horus, and Anubis.
In Hindu tradition, mythological stories are deeply woven into epics such as the Ramayana and Mahabharata.
The Popol Vuh preserves important creation stories and heroic tales from the K'iche' Maya tradition.
Many mythologies explain natural events like thunder, seasons, eclipses, floods, and the changing of day and night.
The phoenix, a bird reborn from ashes, appears in several mythic traditions and later became a universal symbol of renewal.
Dragons appear in mythologies around the world, but they are not all treated the same — some traditions see them as wise or divine rather than evil.
The hero's journey pattern found in many myths was popularized in modern scholarship by Joseph Campbell.
The Minotaur of Greek mythology lived in the Labyrinth on Crete and was ultimately slain by Theseus.
Medusa was one of the Gorgons, and in later retellings she became one of mythology's most recognizable tragic monsters.
Prometheus is the Titan who, in Greek myth, stole fire for humanity and suffered eternal punishment for it.
In Norse mythology, Thor's hammer Mjolnir returns to his hand after being thrown and symbolizes divine protection.
The names of several weekdays in English come from mythological figures, including Thursday from Thor and Wednesday from Odin (Woden).
Arthurian legend blends history, folklore, and mythology through figures like King Arthur, Merlin, and the Knights of the Round Table.
Japanese mythology includes the sun goddess Amaterasu, one of the most important deities in Shinto tradition.
Celtic mythology features heroes like Cú Chulainn and legendary places such as Tir na nÓg.
Many constellations, including Orion, Andromeda, and Perseus, are named after characters from Greek mythology.
The Trojan War, though legendary, became one of the foundational stories of classical mythology through Homer's Iliad.
Mythological stories were often preserved through oral tradition for centuries before being written down.
Trickster figures such as Loki, Anansi, and Coyote appear in many mythic systems and often break rules to force change.
Mythology continues to influence modern fantasy, comics, movies, brand names, and even space missions.
The word 'myth' is often used casually to mean something false, but in scholarship it refers to a culturally meaningful sacred or traditional story.
Joseph Campbell's work on comparative mythology influenced creators from George Lucas to many fantasy writers.
Some mythic creatures, like griffins and mermaids, appear in many different cultures with surprisingly similar imagery.
World myths frequently include flood narratives, underworld journeys, divine battles, and stories of transformation.
Mythology is still a living part of many cultures, religions, and storytelling traditions rather than only an ancient subject.
Athena, goddess of wisdom and war strategy, was said to have sprung fully grown from the head of Zeus.
The story of Persephone's annual return from the underworld was used in Greek myth to explain the cycle of the seasons.
Heracles, known to the Romans as Hercules, became famous for completing twelve near-impossible labors.
Achilles' weak spot, his heel, became one of mythology's most enduring symbols for a hidden vulnerability.
Odysseus blinded the Cyclops Polyphemus during his long voyage home in the Odyssey.
Cerberus, the three-headed hound of Hades, guarded the entrance to the Greek underworld.
The Hydra was a serpent-like monster whose heads grew back when they were cut off.
The Sphinx is famous for posing a deadly riddle that only Oedipus answered correctly.
The Fates of Greek mythology — Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos — spun, measured, and cut the thread of each human life.
The Muses were divine patrons of the arts and sciences in Greek mythology.
Pandora is remembered for opening a jar — often mistranslated as a box — and unleashing suffering into the world.
Icarus flew too close to the sun with wings of wax and feathers, becoming a classic warning against overconfidence.
In Norse mythology, the wolf Fenrir is destined to break free at Ragnarok and kill Odin.
Jormungandr, the Midgard Serpent, is so huge in Norse myth that it encircles the world and bites its own tail.
Yggdrasil, the world tree, links the Nine Realms in Norse mythology.
Valkyries choose which fallen warriors are brought to Valhalla in Norse tradition.
Anansi the spider is a famous trickster figure in West African and Caribbean folklore and mythology.
Coyote plays a similar trickster role in many Native American myth traditions, where he is both foolish and transformative.
Amaterasu, the Shinto sun goddess, once hid in a cave, and the world was plunged into darkness until she emerged.
Quetzalcoatl, the feathered serpent, is one of the best-known deities in Mesoamerican mythology.
In Egyptian myth, Anubis is associated with mummification and guiding souls through the afterlife.
The weighing of the heart in Egyptian mythology determined whether a soul was worthy to enter the afterlife.
Baba Yaga from Slavic folklore lives in a hut that stands on chicken legs and can be helper, villain, or both.
Selkies in Celtic and northern folklore are seal beings who can shed their skins and appear in human form.
Mythic stories often survive because they answer emotional questions about fate, identity, love, death, and the unknown.
Ariadne's thread helped Theseus navigate the Labyrinth after facing the Minotaur.
The tale of Orpheus and Eurydice is one of mythology's most famous underworld love stories.
Pegasus, the winged horse, sprang from the blood of Medusa in Greek myth.
Atlas was condemned to hold up the heavens, which is why his name became linked to bearing the weight of the world.
King Midas's golden touch became a timeless warning about greed and wish fulfillment.
The sirens of Greek mythology lured sailors with irresistible song rather than brute force.
The Norns of Norse mythology play a role similar to the Greek Fates, shaping destiny near the roots of Yggdrasil.
Baldr's death in Norse myth is one of the great tragedies that foreshadows Ragnarok.
Sleipnir, Odin's eight-legged horse, is one of the strangest and most memorable creatures in Norse mythology.
In Hindu mythology, Hanuman is a monkey god celebrated for devotion, strength, and courage.
Rama and Sita are central figures in the Ramayana, one of the most influential epics in world mythology.
The Japanese storm god Susanoo is famous for slaying the serpent Yamata no Orochi.
The Chinese Monkey King Sun Wukong remains one of the most beloved mythic heroes in East Asian storytelling.
The Journey to the West blends mythology, folklore, religion, and fantasy around the adventures of Sun Wukong and his companions.
The rainbow serpent appears in many Aboriginal Australian traditions and is associated with creation, water, and life.
Mythology endures because every culture finds its own way to turn mystery into story and story into meaning.
Zeus became the king of the gods in Greek mythology and ruler of the sky and thunder.
He is one of the first names many people think of when mythology is mentioned.
Zeus is one of the standout topics people mention when discussing Mythology.
Any overview of Mythology usually includes Zeus.
Zeus helped define the legacy and reputation of Mythology.
Fans exploring Mythology are often encouraged to learn about Zeus.
The story of Mythology is hard to explain without mentioning Zeus.
Retrospectives and fan discussions about Mythology frequently return to Zeus.
For many fans, Zeus captures part of why Mythology stays so memorable.
Lists, articles, and guides about Mythology often highlight Zeus.

Greek mythology became one of the most widely studied and retold myth traditions in the world.
Its gods, heroes, and monsters shaped literature, art, and storytelling for centuries.
Greek mythology is one of the standout topics people often explore within Mythology.
Readers interested in Mythology regularly come across Greek mythology.
Learning about Greek mythology helps explain part of the lasting appeal of Mythology.
Greek mythology remains one of the most recognizable examples connected with Mythology.
Articles, documentaries, and fan discussions about Mythology often highlight Greek mythology.
For many people, Greek mythology is a memorable entry point into Mythology.
The story of Greek mythology helps show why Mythology stays so interesting to revisit.
Greek mythology is frequently mentioned in guides and retrospectives about Mythology.
One reason Mythology feels rich and memorable is the presence of touchpoints like Greek mythology.
Greek mythology still stands out as a classic reference point within Mythology.
